sources.full: Descendants of Joseph and Elizabeth (Talcott) Wadsworth — later publication
Descendants of Joseph and Elizabeth (Talcott) Wadsworth — later publication

narrative_blocks.header: Tallmadge's junior officer became Ohio's militia general
narrative_blocks.body: Elijah Wadsworth served through the Revolution under Benjamin Tallmadge, ending the war a captain. Decades later, on the Western Reserve, he was commissioned major-general of the Ohio militia. The frontier soldier had become the frontier's general.

narrative_blocks.header: The house he built became Harriet Beecher Stowe's
narrative_blocks.body: The frame house Wadsworth raised at Canfield in 1803 was the first in the township. Generations later it passed into the hands of the Beecher family. The pioneer's homestead outlived the pioneer by a long way.

narrative_blocks.header: An original signer of the Connecticut Land Company
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1795 Wadsworth was among the signers of the contract that opened the Western Reserve to settlement. He spent the summer of 1799 scouting Canfield and the surrounding towns. By 1802 he had moved his whole family west by horse and ox team.

narrative_blocks.header: He held the frontier after Hull's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: When war was declared in 1812 and General Hull surrendered Detroit, Wadsworth was called into active service as a major-general. He threw his full energy into defending the exposed Ohio frontier. Contemporaries credited him with a real service to the country.

narrative_blocks.header: A whole Ohio city now bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: Wadsworth, Ohio, in Medina County, is named for the general. He had once owned a significant portion of the land on which the city grew. His name outlasted his fortune, which the Revolution had consumed entirely.

cross_connections.display_label: Served under Tallmadge in Sheldon's Horse — both ended up in the Western Reserve
Harriet Beecher Stowe was born in 1811 in the Litchfield house Wadsworth built, after it passed to her father Rev. Lyman Beecher.
largest buyer of that township; Canfield, Ohio named for him

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Connecticut Land Company; agent for Canfield Township 1799-1802.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built it in 1774; sold to Lyman Beecher in 1810 — Harriet Beecher Stowe was born there in 1811.
The 1799 Litchfield house he built, now the Oliver Wolcott Library.

military_service.notes: Served directly under Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge. Co-founded Sheldon's Horse. Served Long Island, Monmouth, Yorktown. Was at West Point when Major André was taken prisoner, 1780.

Commanded Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) as aide. Organized 3,000-man militia; built forts and roads across NE Ohio. Commanded at Battle of the Peninsula, Sep 10, 1812. Resigned Feb 1814 due to age/health. Personal debt of $26,551 supplying troops — Congress discharged 1825.

career.notes: Recalled to active service in the War of 1812

sources.full: Descendants of Joseph and Elizabeth (Talcott) Wadsworth — later publication

notable.notable_blurb: Captain of the Charter Oak legend

narrative_blocks.header: He vanished into the dark with the charter
narrative_blocks.body: By tradition, when the candles went out in the Hartford meeting-house on the night of 31 October 1687, Wadsworth carried the colony's charter into the dark and hid it in a great oak on the Wyllys grounds. Historians note the evidence is thin and the duplicate may have gone to his own cellar, but the legend made his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant in King Philip's War, captain after
narrative_blocks.body: Wadsworth served as a lieutenant during King Philip's War in the 1670s and had risen to captain by 1690. The colony long remembered him as a soldier as much as the charter's keeper.

narrative_blocks.header: Three marriages, but a single line of children
narrative_blocks.body: Wadsworth married three times: first to Elizabeth Barnard, who left no children, and last to the widow Mary Blackleach, who outlived him. All his children came through his second wife, Elizabeth Talcott, daughter of Lieutenant-Colonel John Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: The colony quietly paid him for the deed
narrative_blocks.body: In 1715, nearly thirty years after the event, Connecticut voted Wadsworth twenty shillings for securing the duplicate charter in a very troublesome season. That grant is the closest thing to a contemporary record of his famous act.

narrative_blocks.header: His father crossed on the Lyon with Talcotts
narrative_blocks.body: Wadsworth's father William came from England aboard the ship Lyon in 1632, in the same migration as the Talcott family whose daughter Joseph would later marry. The two Hartford founding houses were thus bound twice over, by voyage and by marriage.

cross_connections.display_label: painted the oak where tradition hid the charter

sources.full: Descendants of Joseph and Elizabeth (Talcott) Wadsworth — later publication

emigration.notes: Came to America in the same ship as John Talcott Sr. (T00011). Made freeman 1632. Came with four children: Sarah, William (died young), Mary, and John.

cross_connections.display_label: William Wadsworth sailed on the Lyon in 1632 with John.

political_offices.title: Deputy / Assistant Magistrate
political_offices.notes: Elected before Connecticut joined New Haven Colony

political_offices.title: Treasurer
political_offices.notes: Succeeded his father May 17, 1660; held office until 1676

political_offices.title: Major, then Lieut.-Colonel
political_offices.notes: Resigned Treasurer and took command of Connecticut army at outbreak of King Philip's War

notable.notable_blurb: Colonial military commander, King Philip's War

narrative_blocks.header: He was named guardian of the charter
narrative_blocks.body: When Connecticut's royal charter arrived from Charles the Second in 1662, the freemen chose Talcott, with Samuel Wyllys and Lieutenant John Allyn, as the committee sworn to keep it in their custody. A quarter-century later he would help spirit that same charter away from Andros.

narrative_blocks.header: He took the charter as the candles died
narrative_blocks.body: By Roger Wolcott's 1764 account, when the candles were snuffed in the Hartford meeting-house in 1687, Talcott and Nathaniel Stanley lifted the two colonial charters from the table before Andros could seize them. Wolcott's telling names no oak; the tree entered the tradition later, and the duplicate passed to his son-in-law Joseph Wadsworth.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut's victorious commander in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott led Connecticut's forces through King Philip's War in 1675 and 1676, winning a string of engagements that earned him promotion to lieutenant-colonel. His campaigns ranged from the river towns down into the Narragansett country.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the colony's purse for sixteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott served as treasurer of Connecticut Colony from 1660 to 1676, taking the office directly from his father before him. The family's account books became a running ledger of early Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: He died intestate, his estate worth thousands
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott died in 1688 without a will, leaving an estate appraised at more than two thousand pounds, a substantial colonial fortune. It passed by primogeniture to his eldest surviving son, the future governor Joseph Talcott.

battles: Multiple engagements with Wampanoag and allied tribes
military_service.notes: Led Connecticut standing army with 200 Mohegan and Pequot Indian allies. 'In the various battles with the Indians in which he was engaged, he was always victorious, and obtained great renown as an Indian fighter.' Promoted from Major to Lieut.-Colonel during the war.

parents.notes: Her father John Wakeman was Colony Treasurer — the same office her husband John Talcott Jr. would later hold
parents.father_research_notes: John Wakeman, Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut
